4 min read
1 hour ago

HomepageWhen people talk about placements, they usually focus on coding interviews, DSA sheets, aptitude tests, or building projects. Those are important, but there is another problem that almost every student quietly deals with.
Keeping your professional profile updated.
Every time I completed a project, learned a new technology, or added another internship, I found myself opening the same five or six websites. Update the resume. Update LinkedIn. Update the portfolio. Update GitHub. Update job portals. Then hope I hadn't forgotten one.
A few weeks later, I'd inevitably discover that my resume had my latest project, my portfolio didn't, and LinkedIn still described a version of me from months ago. None of it was intentional. It was just exhausting to keep everything in sync.
The strange part is that we've somehow accepted this as normal.
Every platform asks for almost the same information. Your education, projects, skills, experience, achievements. Yet we manually type the same details over and over again in slightly different formats. It's repetitive, time-consuming, and honestly feels like a problem software should have solved years ago.
Eventually, maybe even smarter ways to keep the rest of your professional presence in sync.
The more I worked on it, the more I realized that this wasn’t just my problem. Almost every student has information scattered across different platforms. GitHub knows about your code. LinkedIn knows about your experience. Your resume has a carefully written summary. Your portfolio has your best projects. None of them actually know about each other.
And because of that, they slowly drift apart.
A recruiter might see one version of your skills on LinkedIn, another version on your resume, and a completely different story on your portfolio. Not because you’re trying to mislead anyone, but because keeping everything updated takes more effort than it should.
That’s the thought that eventually led me to build Skopia.
https://skopia.in
The idea is simple.
Instead of maintaining your information across multiple places, you maintain it once.
From that single profile, Skopia generates both a personal portfolio website and an ATS-friendly resume that you can keep updating as your career grows.
The goal isn’t to become just another portfolio builder. It’s to become the place where your professional profile actually lives.
What's available today?
After months of building it during evenings and weekends, the first version is now live.
Current features include:
- Create a portfolio website in minutes.
- Generate ATS-friendly resumes from the same profile.
- Every section is optional, so you only showcase what you want.
- Public portfolio website for every user.
- Three production-ready themes (one default and two additional themes).
- Responsive design for both desktop and mobile.
- Live desktop and mobile portfolio previews while editing.
- Zoom support for easier portfolio previewing.
- Update your profile anytime and instantly regenerate your portfolio and resume.
- Email verification.
- Forgot password and password reset using Email OTP.
- Resume sharing links for easier distribution.
The project is built using React, Spring Boot, MongoDB, Cloudinary, and Docker.
What's next?
I'm even more excited about the features currently under development.
The biggest goal is reducing the effort required to maintain your professional presence across different platforms.
I'm currently working on:
- Resume, LinkedIn, and job portal data synchronization.
- Multiple resume and portfolio versions for different roles.
- More portfolio themes.
- Portfolio analytics.
- Auto-save drafts.
- A browser extension that detects when you're on platforms like LinkedIn, GitHub, or job portals and helps auto-fill information directly from your Skopia profile.
Since most of these platforms don't provide APIs for updating user profiles, the browser extension feels like the most practical solution. Instead of copying and pasting the same information repeatedly, your profile could simply be available wherever you're editing it.
That's the long-term vision.
I don't want Skopia to be another website people visit once, generate a resume, and forget about. I want it to become the single source of truth for your professional profile.
There's still a lot to build, and I'm sure there are plenty of ideas I haven't thought of yet.
If you're a student, recent graduate, or someone who's gone through placements, I'd genuinely love your feedback.
- Would you actually use something like this?
- What's missing?
- What would make it genuinely useful during placements or job applications?
You can try it here:
https://skopia.in
It's been built entirely after work and on weekends, one feature at a time. So don't hold back. The most useful feedback is usually the brutally honest kind.
